one_line_takeaway: In hybrid vector+knowledge-graph RAG, the entity-linking step used for graph expansion silently bypasses vector-layer tenant/sensitivity authorization, causing ~95% of ordinary (non-adversarial) queries to leak cross-tenant data at exactly 2 hops -- but a single per-hop authorization re-check at the graph expansion boundary eliminates all measured leakage with negligible latency cost.
key_finding: The undefended hybrid pipeline (P3) exhibits RPR = 0.954 [95% CI 0.931-0.974] on 350 benign queries and RPR = 0.947 [0.907-0.980] on 150 adversarial queries, versus RPR = 0.0 for vector-only retrieval (P1) on the same queries -- meaning 95% of benign, non-adversarial queries leak cross-tenant/over-clearance content purely as a structural byproduct of graph expansion, with no injection required. Mean Leakage@k is 16.0 (benign) and 19.4 (adversarial) items out of a ~110-item context; the regularized Amplification Factor AF(epsilon=0.1) is 160-194x. All observed leakage occurs at exactly Pivot Depth = 2 hops across all 476 leaking queries and across all three corpora (synthetic RPR=0.954, Enron RPR=0.695 on 200 queries/139 leaking, EDGAR RPR=0.085 on 200 queries/17 leaking), which the authors attribute to a structural invariant of bipartite chunk-entity graph topology rather than a corpus-specific artifact; RPR correlates with cross-tenant entity connectivity density (40 bridge entities in synthetic vs. 19 in Enron vs. 13 in EDGAR). All four non-adaptive attacks (A1-A4, injection budgets 9-20 chunks) achieve RPR=1.0 against P3 but RPR=0.0 against D1-defended pipelines (P4/P6/P8); three adaptive attacks (A5-A7, including metadata forgery up to 10%) also fall to RPR=0.0 under D1. A single defense -- D1, per-hop re-authorization (tenant match AND sensitivity <= clearance) applied to every expanded node -- reduces RPR from 0.95 to 0.0 across all three corpora, all query types, and all seven attack variants, with <1ms added latency (P3 p50 26.7ms to P4 26.5ms) while retaining 5.6x more context than vector-only retrieval (56 vs. 10 items).
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes a new attack surface in hybrid RAG systems that combine vector similarity search with knowledge-graph expansion: the 'pivot boundary' where a vector-retrieved seed chunk is mapped (via NER entity linking) into the knowledge graph and BFS-expanded without re-checking authorization. Because entity nodes in these graphs are typically 'tenant-neutral' (carry no ownership metadata), a graph traversal can silently cross tenant/sensitivity boundaries even though vector retrieval itself is fully authorized. The authors formalize this as Retrieval Pivot Risk (RPR) plus companion metrics (Leakage@k, severity-weighted leakage SWL, Amplification Factor AF(epsilon), Pivot Depth PD), define a taxonomy of four non-adaptive attacks (A1 Seed Steering, A2 Entity Anchor Injection, A3 Neighborhood Flooding, A4 Bridge Node Attack) plus three adaptive attacks targeting defenses (A5 metadata forgery, A6 entity manipulation, A7 query manipulation), and propose/evaluate a five-layer defense stack (D1 per-hop authorization, D2 edge allowlist, D3 budgeted traversal, D4 trust-weighted expansion, D5 merge-time policy filter). They build and release a full experimental pipeline (ChromaDB + Neo4j, spaCy NER, sentence-transformer embeddings) and validate on a synthetic multi-tenant enterprise corpus plus two real-world corpora (Enron emails, SEC EDGAR 10-K filings).
threat_model: Multi-tenant enterprise hybrid RAG system: a ChromaDB vector store (tenant/sensitivity metadata, enforces tenant prefilter before retrieval) feeding a Neo4j knowledge graph (entity nodes, chunk nodes, typed edges MENTIONS/DEPENDS_ON/BELONGS_TO/RELATED_TO) that is BFS-expanded to depth d=2 via apoc.path.spanningTree. Two attacker models are considered: (1) an 'injection attacker' holding a legitimate account in one tenant who can inject 10-20 crafted chunks through normal ingestion channels (wiki edits, tickets, shared docs) that undergo standard chunking/NER/embedding/indexing; (2) a 'no-injection attacker' who holds a legitimate account and simply crafts queries mentioning naturally shared entities (vendors, infrastructure, personnel, compliance standards) to trigger organic cross-tenant pivots with zero document injection. Attacker goals: G1 cross-tenant access, G2 sensitivity-tier escalation, G3 amplified leakage (AF>1) versus vector-only retrieval. Scope is explicitly limited to the retrieval layer (unauthorized items reaching the context window), not generation/jailbreaking/prompt injection; the attacker has no direct DB access and cannot modify model weights or pipeline configuration. Adaptive-attacker considerations (metadata spoofing, same-tenant escalation) are discussed and tested (A5-A7).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) all three corpora use a bipartite chunk-entity topology producing a uniform PD=2 signature; richer entity-to-entity graphs (ontological hierarchies, multi-hop inference chains) may show deeper pivot depths, and they do not evaluate production graphs with real RBAC/ABAC policies. (2) Generation-contamination evaluation has small/uneven sample sizes (n=40-181 leaking queries depending on corpus/model) and the GPT-4o synthetic sample (n=10) predates the expanded query pool; chain-of-thought reasoning effects are untested. (3) Only two open-source embedding models tested (MiniLM, MPNet); commercial embeddings (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-3-large) untested, though authors argue the vulnerability is structural (entity-linking-based) rather than embedding-quality-dependent. (4) D1's entity-node filtering is 'functionally equivalent to disabling entity traversal' -- the authors explicitly acknowledge this as a valid criticism and describe a finer-grained entity-aware authorization scheme (per-tenant entity labeling) as future work rather than something they implemented/evaluated. (5) Adaptive attacks tested (A5-A7) do not include attackers who craft high-provenance payloads specifically to bypass D4, or adversarial query phrasing to manipulate D2's query classifier; metadata forgery assumes the attacker can relabel node properties but not modify graph schema/index structure. (6) NER dependence: all corpora use spaCy en_core_web_sm, which has known recall limitations; effect of higher-recall/production entity linkers on RPR is not evaluated. (7) The authorization model is a simplified flat tenant + 4-tier sensitivity scheme; generalization to real RBAC/ABAC, group memberships, temporary grants, and legal holds is asserted but not experimentally demonstrated. (8) Single-author paper (independent researcher, perfecxion.ai), not peer-reviewed at time of this text (references a 2026 arXiv posting with v3 dated March 2026); several cited related works are themselves very recent (2025-2026) arXiv preprints or 'to appear' venues, so this is a fast-moving, largely unreviewed literature.

datasets_used: Synthetic multi-tenant enterprise corpus: 1,000 documents, 4 tenants (acme_engineering, globex_finance, initech_hr, umbrella_security), 2,785 retrievable nodes (785 entity + 2,000 chunk), 15,514 edges, 40 bridge entities across 5 categories

Enron email corpus: subsampled to 50,000 emails from ~150 employees, 5 department-tenants, 376,000 nodes (174,000 entity + 152,000 chunk + 50,000 document), 2.3M edges, 19 naturally shared cross-department entities
SEC EDGAR 10-K filings: 887 sections across 20 companies, 4 sector-tenants, 19,527 nodes (887 documents + 3,692 chunks + 14,948 entities), 427,415 edges, 13 cross-sector bridge entities
quantitative_results: P3 (undefended hybrid) RPR = 0.954 [95% CI 0.931, 0.974] on 350 benign queries; RPR = 0.947 [0.907, 0.980] on 150 adversarial queries
P1 (vector-only) RPR = 0.000 on all query sets across all three corpora
Mean Leakage@k for P3: 16.0 (benign), 19.4 (adversarial); severity-weighted leakage: 22.9 (benign), 26.4 (adversarial)
Regularized Amplification Factor AF(epsilon=0.1) = 160-194x for synthetic corpus
All leakage occurs at Pivot Depth = 2 (min=median=max=2) across 476 leaking queries in synthetic corpus, and PD=2 persists identically on Enron and EDGAR
Cross-dataset RPR: synthetic 0.954, Enron 0.695 (139/200 queries leak, mean 7.1 leaked items), EDGAR 0.085 (17/200 queries leak, mean 0.4 leaked items)
D1 (per-hop authorization) reduces RPR to 0.000 on all three corpora (synthetic, Enron, EDGAR), all query types, and all seven attack variants (A1-A7)
D1 adds <1ms latency (P3 p50 26.7ms -> P4 26.5ms); full defense stack P8 is faster than undefended P3 (22.7ms vs 26.7ms p50)
D1 retains 56 authorized items per query (5.6x vector-only's 10 items); full 5-defense stack (P8) reduces context to 19-20 items (82% reduction from undefended baseline)
Connectivity sweep: RPR stable at 0.93-0.95 regardless of bridge-entity count (0 to 40); mean leakage scales from 21.5 (0 bridges) to 34.5 (40 bridges), a 60% increase
Embedding-model sensitivity: switching MiniLM-L6 (384d) to MPNet (768d) increases RPR from 0.954->0.994 (benign) and 0.947->0.980 (adversarial)
All four non-adaptive attacks (A1-A4, injection budgets 9-20 chunks) achieve RPR=1.0 against P3 and RPR=0.0 against D1-defended pipelines (P4/P6/P8), each producing ~20.5 mean Leakage@k against P3
D1 maintains RPR=0.0 under random sensitivity metadata mislabeling up to 5%
Entity Contamination Rate reaches 0.32 (Claude Sonnet 4.5, synthetic corpus) in the generation-level contamination experiment
Traversal parameter sweep (27 configurations): total_nodes <= 25 eliminates leakage even without D1; depth must be >=2 for the pivot to occur
baselines_compared: P1: Vector-only retrieval with tenant prefilter (used as the zero-leakage security baseline)
P3: Undefended hybrid (vector + graph expansion, no additional defenses) -- the vulnerable baseline
P4-P8: Hybrid with progressively layered defenses D1 (per-hop authz) through D1-D5 (full stack: + edge allowlist + budgeted traversal + trust weighting + merge filter)

Cited (not re-implemented) prior attack baselines used only for injection-budget comparison: PoisonedRAG (90% ASR, 5 injected documents), GRAGPoison (98% ASR via relation-centric poisoning), CorruptRAG, CtrlRAG, TKPA (93.1% success modifying 0.06% of corpus text)
